audit_items.proof_trace: `size_t* state = new size_t[L+1]` is allocated at line 27, but the early return at line 32 (`if (in[i].size() == 0) return {}`) exits without calling `delete[] state`, leaking L+1 size_t values per call. The only `delete[] state` is at line 67 on the normal exit path; no RAII or try/finally guards the raw pointer. The same defect exists in `permutateAlt()` at line 84.
audit_items.attack_scenario: An attacker supplies SCSS input containing `@extend` rules or nested selectors that produce an empty selector group; libsass passes this through `extendCompound`/`weaveParents` into `permutate()`, triggering the early return and leaking heap memory on every such parse. In a server-side context (e.g., node-sass behind a web endpoint), repeated requests accumulate leaks leading to memory exhaustion.
